title: Rabbit respiratory infections: early signs and vet referral
subtitle: Recognising pasteurellosis, snuffles, and stressed breathing before symptoms worsen.
excerpt: Respiratory infections are common in rabbits kept in damp or poorly ventilated housing. Catching the early signs of pasteurellosis and snuffles gives the rabbit the best chance of recovery with treatment.
sourceNotes: Merck Veterinary Manual, rabbit respiratory disease section. RWAF health guides. BSAVA Manual of Rabbit Medicine and Surgery. House Rabbit Society snuffles information.

glossaryTerms.definition: A bacterial infection caused by Pasteurella multocida. It is the most common cause of respiratory disease in rabbits. It can also cause abscesses, ear infections, and reproductive problems. Spread by direct contact and airborne droplets.

The common name for chronic upper respiratory infection in rabbits, most often caused by Pasteurella multocida. Named for the snuffling sound produced by nasal discharge. Can become chronic and difficult to clear completely.

Fluid running from one or both nostrils. Clear discharge can indicate a mild irritation or early infection. White, yellow, or thick discharge indicates active bacterial infection and requires prompt vet attention.

content.text: Rabbits breathe only through their noses. Any obstruction or infection in the nasal passages causes immediate distress. Respiratory infections spread rapidly in groups. Acting at the first sign reduces the risk of permanent damage or flock-wide disease.
Pasteurellosis
Pasteurellosis

is caused by the bacterium Pasteurella multocida. Many rabbits carry the organism without showing symptoms. Stress, overcrowding, damp housing, or a compromised immune system can trigger an active infection. It spreads between rabbits through direct nose contact and shared air.

Early signs include sneezing, clear nasal discharge, and wet fur on the insides of the front legs where the rabbit wipes its nose. As infection progresses, discharge becomes white or yellow and the rabbit breathes faster and more noisily.
Snuffles and its progression
Snuffles

is the name given to chronic upper respiratory infection. A rabbit with snuffles sneezes frequently and has a wet nose. The infection can enter the inner ear and cause head tilt (torticollis). It can also track into the lower respiratory tract and cause pneumonia.

Snuffles is treatable but often not curable. Long-term antibiotic courses reduce symptoms but the bacterium may remain dormant. Many rabbits live comfortably with well-managed snuffles as long as their housing is dry and well-ventilated and their stress levels are low.
Nasal discharge: what to look for
Check the nostrils and inner forelegs at every handling.
Nasal discharge
should be absent entirely in a healthy rabbit. Any discharge warrants attention:
Clear, watery: could be early soreness or a mild cold. Watch closely for a few days.
White or creamy: bacterial infection active. Vet appointment needed.
Yellow or green: significant infection. Vet needed promptly.
Discharge from one nostril only: may indicate an abscess or blockage rather than a systemic infection.
Stressed breathing

A healthy rabbit breathes 30 to 60 times per minute at rest. Faster breathing, visible chest movement, nostril flaring, or a blue tint to the gums (cyanosis) indicates respiratory distress. This is a vet emergency.
In hot weather, a panting or mouth-breathing rabbit is overheating. Move it to shade and cool the ears with cool (not cold) water while arranging emergency vet care.
Isolation and referral
Isolate any rabbit with respiratory symptoms from the group immediately. Use separate feed and water containers. Wash your hands between handling the sick rabbit and others.

When you contact the vet, note: which nostril is affected (one or both), the colour and consistency of the discharge, how long symptoms have been present, and whether any other rabbits are showing signs. This information speeds up diagnosis and treatment decisions.
